region_of_origin: Canada (invented by Sam Panopoulos, 1962); widely consumed globally including Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pizza as a category; no Hawaiian pizza-specific code exists. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is a medium 8-inch Hawaiian pizza (approximately 400 g, 6 slices), providing around 816 kcal per pizza.

nutrient_source.title: Pizza, cheese, with fruit topping — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170459) and HPB Singapore Foodo (composite)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hawaiian pizza (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (5.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.13 g/100g), and vitamin C (7.5 mg/100g) were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Pizza, cheese, with fruit topping' (FDC ID 170459) as HPB did not report these fields. This ensures all nutrient data comes from approved sources. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 as not reported in either HPB or USDA for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pizza with cheese and tomato sauce analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hawaiian pizza found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 80 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'Pizza, cheese and tomato' (GI ~80, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The high sugar content from pineapple topping (15.75 g/100g) and refined wheat dough base support a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (80 × 129.52) / 100 = 103.6, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 32.38 × 4 = 129.52 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Processed meat sodium from cooked ham and high sugar load (15.75 g/100g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Limit to 1–2 slices per sitting; request reduced ham or substitute with fresh chicken to lower processed-meat sodium, and choose reduced-sugar tomato sauce to mitigate insulin-mediated sodium retention associated with the high sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (15.75 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) on a refined wheat dough base producing a high glycemic index (~80) and very high glycemic load per serving (~104)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hawaiian pizza is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~80), very high GL per medium pizza (~104), and sugar content of 15.75 g/100g with only 1.7 g/100g fibre; if consumed, limit to 1 slice, pair with a high-fibre salad, request a wholemeal crust, and use fresh rather than canned pineapple to reduce sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g (24.5% of total fat), trans fat at 0.13 g/100g, and processed cooked ham contributing to atherogenic lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat (5.0 g/100g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100g) elevate LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 1 slice, request reduced-fat cheese, and substitute cooked ham with a lean unprocessed protein to reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cooked ham (processed meat with nitrates) and high sugar (15.75 g/100g) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; tomato sauce is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute cooked ham with fresh chicken to avoid processed-meat nitrates, use fresh pineapple instead of canned to reduce sugar-driven inflammation, and monitor joint symptoms if sensitive to nightshade ingredients in tomato sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hawaiian Pizza is a baked flatbread pizza featuring a hand-stretched, fermented wheat dough base topped with tomato sauce, mozzarella cheese, cooked ham, and pineapple chunks. The dish is widely attributed to Sam Panopoulos, a Greek-Canadian restaurateur who created it in Ontario, Canada in 1962, though it has since become a globally popular pizza variant served extensively in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The crust is characteristically soft and chewy from the fermentation process, with a slightly crisp underside. The flavour profile is a sweet-savoury contrast: the pineapple (often canned in syrup) provides tropical sweetness and acidity, the ham contributes saltiness and umami, and the melted mozzarella adds creaminess. Per 100 g, Hawaiian pizza provides approximately 204 kcal, 1.84 g protein, 7.53 g fat (of which 5.0 g is saturated), 32.38 g carbohydrate, 15.75 g sugar, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 130 mg sodium. The high sugar content (largely from pineapple and tomato sauce) combined with low fibre and a refined dough base results in a high glycemic index (~80) and a very high glycemic load per medium pizza (~104). Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation. The saturated fat and trans fat content (0.13 g/100g) present moderate cardiovascular concern. Sodium from processed ham warrants caution for hypertensive individuals. Pineapple provides a modest 7.5 mg/100g vitamin C. In Singapore, Hawaiian pizza is commonly served as a lunch or dinner option at casual dining restaurants and pizza delivery chains.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of Hawaiian pizza?
Is Hawaiian pizza high in sugar?
What is the glycemic index of Hawaiian pizza?
Is Hawaiian pizza suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Pizza, cheese, with fruit topping' (FDC ID 170459). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cheese and tomato pizza, GI ~80) rather than a direct measurement for Hawaiian pizza. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Hawaiian pizza not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pizza entries as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB or USDA for this specific preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Hawaiian pizza and are not available in a directly matching USDA SR Legacy entry as of 2026. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Japanese', which appears to be a data entry error; Hawaiian pizza is a Western dish of Canadian origin widely served in Singapore. Cuisine has been corrected to 'Western'. The input protein value of 1.84 g/100g is notably low for a pizza with ham and cheese; this is accepted as reported by HPB but flagged for future review against a direct HPB source lookup. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
